question_text: In consultative selling, the most important shift from product-oriented selling is:

options.a: Selling at a higher price point than competitors.
options.b: Focusing on quarterly sales targets and pipeline.
options.c: Shifting from 'what we offer' to 'what they need'.
options.d: Using more case studies in every customer meeting.

question_text: Research consistently shows that the cost of acquiring a new customer compared to retaining an existing customer is roughly:

options.a: Less expensive due to economies of scale in acquisition.
options.b: 5–7 times more expensive to acquire than to retain.
options.c: About twice as expensive to acquire as to retain.
options.d: About the same when measured over a full sales cycle.

question_text: Net Promoter Score (NPS) is calculated as:

options.a: Total number of Promoters divided by all the respondents.
options.b: Simple average of all responses on the 0–10 rating scale.
options.c: Percentage of Promoters minus Percentage of Detractors.
options.d: Total number of repeat customers within a single year.

question_text: In key account management, 'whitespace analysis' refers to:

options.a: Identifying untapped opportunities within an existing account.
options.b: Studying the customer base of your strongest direct competitor.
options.c: Analysing the unused capacity in your active sales pipeline.
options.d: Analysing time gaps between repeat customer transactions.

question_text: RFM customer segmentation analyses three dimensions:

options.a: Reach, Funnel, Marketing spend.
options.b: Region, Function, Market segment.
options.c: Recency, Frequency, Monetary value.
options.d: Revenue, Forecast, Margin level.

question_text: Clayton Christensen's 'Jobs to be Done' framework reframes customer needs as:

options.a: Stages of the sales funnel the customer moves through.
options.b: Product features the customer would like to see added.
options.c: The job the customer wants done in a situation.
options.d: Demographic categories describing who the customer is.

question_text: If a sales rep has a 10% close rate (lead-to-customer), and the average customer is worth ₹2 lakhs, what is the average revenue value of a single lead?
A 'weighted pipeline' forecast multiplies each opportunity's value by:

options.a: Its expected probability of closing.
options.b: The time already spent on the deal.
options.c: The number of stakeholders involved.
options.d: The salesperson's individual quota.

question_text: In modern B2C sales, 'product-led growth' refers to:

options.a: Product roadmaps prioritising growth-oriented features.
options.b: Letting the product drive acquisition and retention.
options.c: Sales driven by aggressive feature development cycles.
options.d: Growth driven by product managers, not by sales teams.

question_text: Research on consumer pricing has consistently shown that 'charm pricing' (e.g., ₹999 instead of ₹1000) works because:

options.a: Odd numbers are aesthetically preferred over even numbers.
options.b: Consumers genuinely cannot do basic mental arithmetic well.
options.c: Left-digit anchoring affects how magnitude is perceived.
options.d: It signals a discount to buyers even when none exists.

question_text: From a unit-economics perspective, when offering a 20% discount, the equivalent volume increase required to maintain the same gross profit (with a 50% gross margin) is approximately:

options.a: 20% volume increase.
options.b: 40% volume increase.
options.c: 67% volume increase.
options.d: 100% volume increase.

question_text: David Maister's 'Trust Equation' for professional relationships is:

options.a: Skill + Track record + Likeability across interactions.
options.b: (Competence + Authority) × Communication consistency.
options.c: Honesty × Competence × Time spent working together.
options.d: (Credibility + Reliability + Intimacy) / Self-focus.

question_text: A long-time customer has just informed you they are leaving for a competitor. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Escalate to your manager to handle the retention conversation on your behalf today.
options.b: Immediately offer a significant discount to retain them and prevent the loss.
options.c: Schedule a call: listen, ask what would solve it, respect their decision.
options.d: Send a formal thank-you email and accept the loss gracefully to preserve goodwill.

question_text: A client says: 'Either reduce your price by 25% or we move our business to a competitor.' The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Ask: "What's behind the 25% — competitor pricing, budget, or something else?" Explore.
options.b: Walk away and let them go to their competitor of choice without further discussion.
options.c: Reduce the price by the full 25% to retain the business and protect the relationship.
options.d: Counter with a 10% discount as a compromise to bridge the pricing gap between you.

question_text: Your main contact at a major client has just left the company. The replacement is unknown. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Continue execution and let the relationship rebuild naturally over the next few months.
options.b: Wait for the replacement to reach out to you when they are settled into their new role.
options.c: Use the relationship with your former contact to set up the introduction to the replacement.
options.d: Email the replacement, request intro meeting, share continuity context, listen to priorities.

question_text: A senior client repeatedly behaves disrespectfully (raising voice, dismissive comments). Your company values the account. The MOST professional response is:

options.a: Schedule a calm 1:1 outside heated moments: name the pattern, set respectful expectations.
options.b: Tolerate it — they're a senior client and the account matters to your company overall.
options.c: Stop engaging with them and let your manager handle the day-to-day relationship instead.
options.d: Mirror their behaviour to assert yourself and signal you won't accept the disrespect.

question_text: You are managing a large account with multiple stakeholders. Two of them — Operations and Finance — have conflicting priorities about your proposal. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Propose two different options and let the two stakeholders debate it amongst themselves.
options.b: Escalate the conflict to their senior leadership to resolve before continuing the work.
options.c: Meet each separately to understand interests, then design a proposal that addresses both.
options.d: Pick the more senior of the two stakeholders and align your proposal with their view.

question_text: Your team is debating whether to raise prices by 8%. Customer surveys show modest sensitivity; revenue is strong. The MOST rigorous approach to the decision is:

options.a: Hold prices — better not to disturb a working revenue model when things are going well.
options.b: Test the increase on a defined segment for 90 days; decide based on the real evidence.
options.c: Raise prices — current strong demand and modest sensitivity supports the proposed increase.
options.d: Survey customers directly about their willingness to pay more for the current product.

question_text: A customer has been using your core product happily for 2 years. You have an adjacent product that could add value. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Run a business review: listen for pain points; share the adjacent product only if it fits.
options.b: Wait for them to ask about your other offerings when they are ready to consider more.
options.c: Send them marketing material about the adjacent product to introduce it to their team.
options.d: Offer a discount on the adjacent product as an incentive to encourage them to trial it.

question_text: Your manager pushes you to forecast aggressively (high probability on uncertain deals). The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Comply with the request — managers are ultimately responsible for forecast accuracy.
options.b: Refuse to provide any forecast at all until the data warrants a clearer projection.
options.c: Use the manager's preferred number publicly but track your real number privately.
options.d: Push back: "My honest read is X. I'd rather under-promise — let me know if different."

question_text: A long-time client requests a service that you don't offer well and would be better served by a competitor. The MOST ethical response is:

options.a: Be honest: "This is outside our strength. [Company X] does it well — I'd recommend them."
options.b: Try to deliver it anyway to retain the relationship and avoid losing them to a rival.
options.c: Decline the request politely without any further explanation to the long-time client.
options.d: Propose an inflated price to discourage them while still keeping the relationship open.

question_text: A multi-year contract is up for renewal in 90 days. The client is satisfied but you suspect a competitor is being evaluated. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Offer a multi-year renewal discount to lock them in before any competitive evaluation.
options.b: Reach out to senior leadership at the client to bypass the buyer team's process.
options.c: Run a business review now: revisit goals, quantify value, surface any evaluation openly.
options.d: Wait for the client to make the first move when they are ready to discuss renewal terms.

question_text: Before a major proposal, the MOST effective preparation is to:

options.a: Polish the deck and rehearse the pitch to deliver a confident professional presentation.
options.b: Lead with your company's credentials and history to establish strong opening credibility.
options.c: Identify the 2-3 things the client cares most about and build the proposal around them.
options.d: Include every possible feature and capability to demonstrate the breadth of your offer.

question_text: You need engineering and finance buy-in for a customer commitment you want to make. They are reluctant. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Drop the opportunity entirely if the internal teams won't support what the customer needs.
options.b: Escalate the request to senior leadership to mandate cooperation from both functions.
options.c: Make the commitment to the customer and figure out internal alignment afterwards as needed.
options.d: Engage both: understand concerns, find a version that works, go to customer with one offer.

question_text: You discover an important pattern in customer feedback that should change how your product is developed. The MOST effective way to communicate this is:

options.a: Send a long email to the product team with all the details of the pattern you found.
options.b: Build a small prototype yourself to demonstrate the need to the product team visually.
options.c: Mention the insight casually in a meeting where the product team happens to be present.
options.d: Create a brief: pattern, evidence, implication, recommendation; meet to debate it.

question_text: Your company is entering a new market segment. As an experienced commercial professional, the MOST effective first 90 days are:

options.a: Run 20-30 discovery conversations; refine positioning and offer; then begin prospecting.
options.b: Begin direct prospecting immediately to generate pipeline and validate market interest fast.
options.c: Build a comprehensive marketing campaign first to generate awareness in the new segment.
options.d: Replicate the playbook from your existing market and adjust as you learn from the segment.

question_text: Your team is significantly below quota with one month to go in the quarter. The MOST effective response as team lead is:

options.a: Offer discounts on existing pipeline to close faster within the remaining time window.
options.b: Ask senior leadership to lower the quota since the team won't be able to meet it now.
options.c: Diagnose with team: where's the leak in the funnel? Targeted intervention on bottleneck.
options.d: Push the team to make more outbound calls to generate volume in the time remaining.

question_text: A junior salesperson on your team consistently loses deals at the proposal stage. The MOST effective coaching intervention is:

options.a: Listen in on next discovery calls, debrief, co-design a proposal together next opportunity.
options.b: Take over their accounts personally for a quarter until they are ready to return to selling.
options.c: Send them to a structured sales training programme to upskill on proposal writing craft.
options.d: Review their proposals and rewrite them so the next ones land better with the customer.

question_text: Your team's CRM hygiene is poor — incomplete data, missed updates. The MOST effective intervention as team lead is:

options.a: Hire an admin to update the CRM for the team so reps can focus on selling not data entry.
options.b: Send a strongly-worded email demanding compliance from the team going forward immediately.
options.c: Make CRM accuracy visible in weekly forecast calls: stale deals can't be discussed there.
options.d: Threaten consequences for non-compliance to make the seriousness clear to all the team.

question_text: You believe you are underpaid relative to your market value and contribution. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Wait for the annual review and hope your compensation gets addressed at that point.
options.b: Schedule a focused conversation: market data, your contributions, what you are asking for.
options.c: Get a competing offer from another company and use it as leverage in the conversation.
options.d: Mention your dissatisfaction broadly to peers to see if others feel the same way too.

question_text: A customer is about to sign a contract for a solution that you suspect doesn't actually fit their need. The MOST ethical response is:

options.a: Reduce the scope of the contract to minimise any potential harm from the wrong solution.
options.b: Pause and ask: "Before we sign, I want to make sure this is right. Here's my concern."
options.c: Close the deal — they made their own decision and they are the ones with the authority.
options.d: Mention the concern only to your manager so they can decide whether to flag it externally.

question_text: To strengthen your position as a trusted advisor, the MOST effective ongoing practice is:

options.a: Develop deep industry expertise: read, build relationships, write or speak publicly.
options.b: Attend more sales training to sharpen your skills in selling and customer engagement.
options.c: Increase the polish and visual quality of your proposals to differentiate your offering.
options.d: Reach out to your customers more frequently to stay top of mind in their day-to-day work.

question_text: I instinctively understand what a customer is really trying to accomplish, even when they describe it imprecisely.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am confident negotiating high-stakes commercial terms with senior decision-makers.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I have deep, specific knowledge about my industry that customers find valuable.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I think about my key accounts strategically (whitespace, stakeholder maps, growth opportunities), not just transactionally.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am comfortable with the commercial math — unit economics, margins, customer LTV, pricing analysis.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can effectively coordinate internal stakeholders (engineering, finance, ops) to deliver for customers.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: After losing a major deal, I can reflect, learn, and move forward without lingering damage to my motivation.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am comfortable investing in customer relationships and pipeline that may take 12+ months to convert.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am willing to walk away from deals that don't fit, even when the revenue would help my numbers.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I enjoy coaching other commercial professionals and can identify what they need to develop.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: When deals are stuck, my first instinct is usually to offer a discount.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I tend to deprioritise time with difficult customers in favour of easier ones.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: How often do you read industry publications, attend industry events, or have conversations with industry peers outside your customer base? PILLAR 02  ·  LEADERSHIP & INFLUENCE Leading teams, coaching, difficult conversations, cross-functional influence, organisational change, and the mid-career leadership behaviours that separate strong individual contributors from credible leaders. Items in this pillar: 45

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: The GROW coaching model (developed by Sir John Whitmore) structures coaching conversations through:

options.a: Growth, Resilience, Outcomes, Wins.
options.b: Goal, Risk, Outcomes, Wins captured.
options.c: Goal, Reality, Options, Will.
options.d: Guide, Review, Optimise, Wrap up.

question_text: Patrick Lencioni's 'Five Dysfunctions of a Team' identifies the foundational dysfunction as:

options.a: Absence of trust.
options.b: Unclear strategy.
options.c: Lack of skill diversity.
options.d: Inadequate resources.

question_text: Adam Grant's research distinguishes 'givers' from 'takers' in workplace relationships. His finding about givers is:

options.a: Givers cluster at both the top and the bottom of outcomes.
options.b: Takers consistently outperform givers in competitive contexts.
options.c: Givers and takers achieve identical career outcomes overall.
options.d: Givers consistently finish last in their career outcomes.

question_text: In Kim Scott's 'Radical Candor' framework, effective feedback combines:

options.a: Strict professionalism with no personal warmth shown.
options.b: Maximum directness regardless of relationship.
options.c: Caring without challenge, to protect the relationship.
options.d: Caring personally and challenging directly.

question_text: Brené Brown's research on leadership identifies vulnerability as:

options.a: Sharing personal details widely across the workplace teams.
options.b: A form of weakness that should be hidden from colleagues.
options.c: An optional quality found in some highly effective leaders.
options.d: The willingness to show up when the outcome is uncertain.

question_text: French and Raven's classic taxonomy of social power identifies five (later six) bases. The ones grounded in personal qualities rather than position are:

options.a: Legitimate and reward power.
options.b: Reward and coercive power.
options.c: Coercive and legitimate power.
options.d: Expert and referent power.

question_text: In 'Crucial Conversations' (Patterson et al.), the recommended first step when a difficult conversation goes wrong is to:

options.a: Push your point more forcefully to keep the conversation alive.
options.b: End the conversation and try again at a later time.
options.c: Step back and restore psychological safety before continuing.
options.d: Apologise and concede ground to keep the relationship intact.

question_text: BJ Fogg's Behaviour Model proposes that behaviour occurs when three elements converge simultaneously:

options.a: Knowledge, Skill, and Desire.
options.b: Motivation, Ability, and Prompt.
options.c: Intention, Capability, and Time.
options.d: Habit, Reward, and Trigger Cue.

question_text: Edwin Locke's goal-setting theory (one of the most empirically supported theories in management) shows that the most effective goals are:

options.a: Vague but inspiring, leaving room for personal interpretation.
options.b: Easy enough that success is virtually guaranteed.
options.c: Privately held to avoid social pressure of any kind.
options.d: Specific and challenging, with clear feedback on progress.

question_text: The distinction between a mentor and a sponsor in career development is:

options.a: Mentors are external to the company; sponsors are always internal.
options.b: Mentor and sponsor describe essentially the same role today.
options.c: Mentors advise you; sponsors actively advocate for your advancement.
options.d: Sponsors are paid for their time; mentors typically work without pay.

question_text: You have just been promoted to manage a team that has been underperforming for 18 months. Your boss expects rapid improvement. The MOST effective first 30 days are:

options.a: Run 1:1s, observe meetings, talk to customers and boss — diagnose root cause first.
options.b: Maintain current operations and observe for 90 days before making any major changes.
options.c: Announce a new strategy and set ambitious targets immediately to drive a step change.
options.d: Replace the lowest performers immediately to signal change and create capacity for new hires.

question_text: You have just been promoted to manage former peers. The MOST effective first conversation with each direct report is:

options.a: Acknowledge change: "Our relationship is shifting. Let's talk about what we each need."
options.b: Continue as if nothing has changed in the relationship to avoid creating awkwardness.
options.c: Avoid one-on-one meetings until the new dynamic between you settles down naturally.
options.d: Establish authority by making clear changes immediately to set the new dynamic up front.

question_text: One of your top-performing team members consistently exhibits behaviour that damages team morale (sarcasm, exclusion, talking over others). The MOST effective action is:

options.a: Move them to a more isolated role where their behaviour affects other team members less.
options.b: Tolerate the behaviour — their high performance is essential to the team's results overall.
options.c: Address it through subtle hints in team meetings to model the behaviour you want from them.
options.d: Direct conversation: name behaviours, share impact, make clear it's non-negotiable now.

question_text: Your boss has set a strategic direction you strongly disagree with. You have already raised your view; the boss has decided. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Maintain professional distance — implement the strategy but don't actively support it.
options.b: Implement the strategy and signal to your team that it wasn't the choice you would have made.
options.c: Commit fully: "The call has been made; my job is to make it succeed. Here's why we do this."
options.d: Continue to lobby internally for your view to try to influence the decision over time.

question_text: Two of your direct reports are in conflict over resource allocation — each believes they need a key team member full-time. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Decide for them yourself based on which of the two initiatives seems more important now.
options.b: Tell them to figure it out between themselves without further involvement from you.
options.c: Bring them together: define criteria, have them present cases, mediate trade-off together.
options.d: Split the resource 50/50 between the two of them to be fair to both initiatives equally.

question_text: Your company has just announced major restructuring. Your team is anxious and rumours are flying. The MOST effective leadership posture is:

options.a: Be present and honest: share what's known, acknowledge what's not, hold space for concerns.
options.b: Share your own uncertainty and worries openly to bond with the team during the upheaval.
options.c: Reassure them that everything will be fine and that there is nothing they need to worry about.
options.d: Avoid the topic of restructuring and focus the team on their day-to-day work instead.

question_text: You are leading a project with team members from multiple departments who don't report to you. Two members are not delivering. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Escalate the issue to their managers immediately to enforce accountability through them.
options.b: Private conversation with each: share observations, agree changes; escalate if needed.
options.c: Take over their work yourself to keep the project on track without confronting the issue.
options.d: Send a strongly worded email to the entire project group to put pressure on the laggards.

question_text: It is annual review time. A direct report has had a strong year overall but with one significant negative incident. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Discuss both with evidence; use the negative incident as development not defining narrative.
options.b: Skip the negative incident because the year was strong overall on most other measures.
options.c: Focus on the strengths and minimise the incident to maintain the team member's morale.
options.d: Lead with the negative incident upfront to make the consequences of it very clear to them.

question_text: You need your team to adopt a major new way of working. You announce it; resistance is significant. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Slow down: listening sessions, address concerns, equip the team, re-launch with their input.
options.b: Abandon the change altogether to maintain team morale and avoid further organisational conflict.
options.c: Implement with the willing team members; let the resisters opt out for now and join later.
options.d: Enforce the change firmly across the team — resistance must be overcome to deliver impact.

question_text: Your team has delivered an exceptional outcome. Senior leadership wants to publicly recognise the work. The MOST effective response as the team's manager is:

options.a: Suggest the recognition go to the most senior members of the team for the achievement.
options.b: Accept the recognition graciously on behalf of the whole team in your role as the manager.
options.c: Name specific team members and contributions publicly, especially the less visible ones.
options.d: Deflect with "It was a team effort" to share the credit equally across the whole team.

question_text: You want to develop a high-potential team member by giving them a stretch assignment. The MOST effective design of this assignment is:

options.a: Give them a task far above their current capability to force rapid growth through challenge.
options.b: Assign them to shadow you on your hardest work so they can learn by close observation.
options.c: Give them more of what they already do well to build on their existing strong base of work.
options.d: Give them something challenging where success is plausible — with criteria, support, freedom.

question_text: Your boss asks you to take on a project that you believe is misaligned with strategic priorities. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Share view: "Before I commit, want to flag a concern. How are you thinking about this?"
options.b: Take it on without comment to maintain a good working relationship with your boss going forward.
options.c: Accept the project but plan to push back on it later once you have more information.
options.d: Refuse the request and explain to your boss exactly why you think it is the wrong call.

question_text: You have an open role. Three candidates are similar in qualifications; two have backgrounds well-represented on your team, one has a background under-represented. The MOST appropriate approach is:

options.a: Evaluate all three on the merits; count team diversity as a meaningful performance contributor.
options.b: Run a final assessment specifically designed to differentiate them objectively on the work.
options.c: Hire the under-represented candidate specifically to improve the diversity of your current team.
options.d: Hire based purely on quantifiable criteria, ignoring background entirely in the decision process.

question_text: You discover that two team members in similar roles have a significant pay gap that you cannot justify on performance grounds. The MOST appropriate action is:

options.a: Quietly adjust the gap at the next compensation review cycle without flagging it explicitly.
options.b: Inform the underpaid employee directly so they can advocate for themselves with HR.
options.c: Raise with HR: present the data, advocate for correction, follow through transparently.
options.d: Maintain the status quo for now — pay history at the company is complicated to address.

question_text: Your team has been running flat-out for 3 months and showing signs of collective exhaustion. Targets are still pressing. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Push the team harder — the targets must be met regardless of how exhausted people feel.
options.b: Step back: cut or defer work; negotiate scope; build in real recovery time for team.
options.c: Hire more people to relieve the load on the existing team members in the short term.
options.d: Allow more flexible working hours to ease the pressure on team members across the week.

question_text: You made a decision 2 months ago that has clearly turned out to be wrong, with team consequences. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Avoid public discussion of the issue to preserve your authority as the team's leader.
options.b: Move forward without dwelling on the mistake — looking back doesn't change the past now.
options.c: Identify contributing factors that led to the wrong decision to share context with the team.
options.d: Acknowledge to team: "I made the call on X. Here's what I missed; here's the plan now."

question_text: A senior leader is repeatedly sending you work requests at 11pm with expectations of next-morning responses. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Address it: "I need to draw a line on after-hours except genuine emergencies. Here's how."
options.b: Respond only during working hours and let the senior leader notice the new pattern over time.
options.c: Comply with the requests but reduce the quality of your output to manage your time better.
options.d: Comply with the requests to maintain a strong working relationship with the senior leader.

question_text: A junior person reaches out asking you to mentor them. You have limited capacity. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Offer a bounded relationship: "30 minutes monthly for 6 months with a specific question."
options.b: Decline politely and let them know you don't have time to take on a mentee right now.
options.c: Suggest they find someone else better suited to mentor them on the topics they care about.
options.d: Accept the request formally — investing in mentorship is important for the next generation.

question_text: A star performer on your team is plateauing. They're still hitting numbers but development has stalled. The MOST effective intervention is:

options.a: Leave them alone — they are still performing well and don't need any additional intervention.
options.b: Increase their compensation to maintain their motivation and reduce the risk of attrition.
options.c: Honest chat: "Your edge is dulling. What's keeping you engaged? Co-design your next stretch."
options.d: Give them more work to keep them challenged and prevent them from getting bored over time.

question_text: After 6 months of clear documented feedback and support, a team member is still not meeting standards. The MOST appropriate next step is:

options.a: Continue providing support indefinitely — give them more time to turn the situation around.
options.b: Push them to resign by making the role miserable so they choose to leave on their own.
options.c: Reduce their responsibilities and hope they stay quietly in a reduced role going forward.
options.d: Direct conversation with HR: this isn't working, here's the path, treat them well in exit.

question_text: I derive genuine satisfaction from developing other people, even when I don't get credit.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am willing to have difficult conversations promptly rather than letting issues accumulate.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can hold strategic patience even when short-term pressure is high.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: When someone gives me critical feedback, I can hear it without immediately defending.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I delegate work effectively, trusting others to deliver in their own way.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I stay composed and effective when those around me are stressed or in crisis.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I make my team's specific contributions visible to senior leadership.

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)

question_text: A standard stakeholder mapping framework plots stakeholders on which two dimensions?

options.a: Friendliness and Difficulty score.
options.b: Power and Interest in the outcome.
options.c: Seniority and Department status.
options.d: Internal status and Tenure level.

question_text: In communicating with senior executives, the strongest opening line is generally:

options.a: The recommendation or insight in a single sentence.
options.b: The background context that sets up the discussion.
options.c: An anecdote chosen to engage their attention quickly.
options.d: A summary of the methodology used in the analysis.

question_text: Cialdini's principle of 'commitment and consistency' suggests that:

options.a: Leaders are preferred when they behave consistently across contexts.
options.b: Commitments must be repeated consistently over a long time.
options.c: Small public commitments lead to larger consistent actions.
options.d: Consistent messaging is more persuasive than varied messaging.

question_text: The 'Hero's Journey' narrative structure, popularised by Joseph Campbell and useful in business storytelling, follows the basic arc:

options.a: Background, action, outcome — a chronological story shape.
options.b: Setup, middle, end — a classical three-part dramatic shape.
options.c: Problem, analysis, recommendation, action — a consulting arc.
options.d: Ordinary world, call, trials, transformation, return.

question_text: The SBI (Situation-Behaviour-Impact) feedback model frames feedback as:

options.a: Skill being assessed, behaviour observed, suggested improvement.
options.b: State of mind, belief held, and inquiry made.
options.c: Strategy in play, behaviour shown, intent inferred.
options.d: Situation, observable behaviour, and the impact it had.

question_text: Chris Voss's negotiation principle of 'tactical empathy' is best described as:

options.a: Concealing your real position to gain a negotiating edge.
options.b: Demonstrating understanding of the other side explicitly.
options.c: Pretending to agree with the other side to build trust.
options.d: Using emotional appeals to win the argument decisively.

question_text: You have 10 minutes with the CEO to brief on a critical project. The MOST effective use of that time is:

options.a: First minute: status, decision needed, biggest risk. Next 6-7: detail. Final: explicit asks.
options.b: Present detailed analysis throughout to demonstrate the rigour of the team's work to date.
options.c: Open with the team's recent accomplishments to set a positive tone for the conversation.
options.d: Walk through the project chronologically from inception through to its current state today.

question_text: You need to email a senior stakeholder about a significant project setback. The MOST effective email structure is:

options.a: Bottom line first; then cause, implications, recovery plan with checkpoints, your ask, offer.
options.b: Comprehensive narrative with the full context first before the recommendation at the end.
options.c: Lead with an apology, then the context, then the recovery plan for the project ahead.
options.d: Brief mention of the issue with an offer to discuss the details verbally at a later time.

question_text: Two senior stakeholders have given you contradictory direction on a project. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Surface conflict to both: "I'm getting different direction. Could we align on this together?"
options.b: Go with the more senior of the two stakeholders to minimise the political risk to yourself.
options.c: Pick the one who escalates more to avoid getting into trouble with the louder stakeholder.
options.d: Average the two pieces of direction to balance both viewpoints in your project's execution.

question_text: A senior leader (not your direct manager) asks you to do work that's outside your remit and would compete with your priorities. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Comply with the request — senior leaders' asks trump your normal priorities for the day.
options.b: Engage warmly: "Let me check with my manager since this affects priorities. Back in 24 hours."
options.c: Take it on and quietly deprioritise existing work to make room for the new senior request.
options.d: Refuse the request — they should know to go through the proper channels for cross-team work.

question_text: You need to deliver tough performance feedback to a long-tenured team member who is sensitive. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Be clear and specific: lead with respect, share evidence, listen, co-design path forward.
options.b: Deliver the feedback via written message to give the team member space to process it alone.
options.c: Soften the feedback significantly to protect their feelings and the long-standing relationship.
options.d: Have HR present in the conversation to formalise the feedback and add appropriate weight.

question_text: In a senior meeting, a peer publicly mischaracterises your team's work. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Send a written correction to all the attendees of the meeting once the session has ended.
options.b: Calmly add factual clarity: "I want to add some context — [specific factual point here]."
options.c: Stay silent during the meeting and address the issue with them privately afterwards.
options.d: Correct them firmly in the moment to defend your team's reputation in front of leadership.

question_text: A team member comes to you upset about a workplace situation. The MOST useful first response is:

options.a: Offer your perspective and advice on what they should do about the situation immediately.
options.b: Ask what kind of support they want: "To vent, to get advice, or for me to do something?"
options.c: Listen quietly without responding so they have the space to fully express their feelings.
options.d: Suggest they take the issue up directly with HR if it is serious enough to warrant action.

question_text: You are giving a major presentation to 200+ people. 5 minutes in, your slide deck fails. The MOST effective in-the-moment response is:

options.a: End the presentation early since the slides are essential to delivering the full message.
options.b: Apologise repeatedly to the audience and ask for the technical issue to be fixed quickly.
options.c: Stay composed, acknowledge briefly with humour, proceed with the content you already know.
options.d: Read your speaker notes directly to the audience until the slides come back online again.

question_text: You've drafted an important email and re-read it. You realise the tone might come across as defensive or sharp. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Send it as is — your point is valid and the recipient should hear it as you wrote it.
options.b: Send a shorter version of the email without the sharp parts but with the main message intact.
options.c: Set it aside for hours; re-read with fresh eyes; rewrite to be clear without being sharp.
options.d: Send with a softening note ("apologies if this comes across strongly to you") at the top.

question_text: You meet someone influential at a conference. The MOST effective way to build a meaningful ongoing relationship is:

options.a: Wait for them to reach out to you if they are interested in building the relationship.
options.b: Connect on LinkedIn with a generic invitation message after the conference ends each day.
options.c: Within 24-48 hours: specific note, share something useful, easy way to stay connected.
options.d: Ask them directly for a follow-up coffee meeting to continue the conversation further.

question_text: Working with a global team, you notice your direct feedback style isn't landing well with members from cultures with less direct communication norms. The MOST effective adaptation is:

options.a: Maintain your own style — they should adapt to professional norms common in the company.
options.b: Have someone from their own culture deliver the feedback on your behalf to them instead.
options.c: Adapt: more warmth, indirectness, face-saving framing; keep content clarity, adjust form.
options.d: Stop giving feedback to those team members to avoid the issue entirely going forward.

question_text: You have important information that contradicts a senior leader's stated position in a meeting. The MOST mature approach is:

options.a: Stay silent — the senior leader may not appreciate being publicly contradicted in front of others.
options.b: Mention the issue privately to the senior leader after the meeting has ended that day.
options.c: Add as data: "I have some recent data that points in a different direction. Worth factoring?"
options.d: Contradict them firmly with the evidence to make sure the right information is on the table.

question_text: A peer has been undermining you in conversations with leadership. You have observed it twice. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Mirror their behaviour by undermining them in conversations with leadership in return.
options.b: Escalate the issue to your shared manager and let them handle the conflict between you.
options.c: Calm direct chat: "In two recent conversations I noticed X. What's going on for you?"
options.d: Confront them privately and demand that the undermining behaviour stop with immediate effect.

question_text: You receive a series of urgent emails throughout the day. The MOST effective email management approach is generally:

options.a: Respond quickly to senior people only and to others whenever it is convenient to do so.
options.b: Respond immediately to every single email to maintain a reputation for being responsive.
options.c: Batch processing into 2-3 dedicated periods per day; protect deep work from interruption.
options.d: Keep email open all day to react to incoming requests in real time as they arrive.

question_text: You're invited to a recurring weekly meeting that you find low-value. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Decline all future invitations to that meeting with a brief note explaining the decision.
options.b: Speak with the owner: "Could we discuss what's useful or whether async updates might work?"
options.c: Continue attending — declining the meeting might signal disengagement to the team's leader.
options.d: Attend but multitask quietly to save time and complete other work in parallel during it.

question_text: You want to build your professional reputation on LinkedIn. The MOST effective long-term strategy is:

options.a: Post inspirational content daily to build a wide following through reach across the platform.
options.b: Comment on every post in your network to maximise visibility across the platform daily.
options.c: Share specific perspectives on your domain — from real work and real lessons, consistently.
options.d: Repost trending business content to associate yourself with what the wider industry discusses.

question_text: In a meeting, a stakeholder becomes openly hostile toward you. The MOST effective in-the-moment response is:

options.a: Stay silent and let them finish what they are saying before responding at the end.
options.b: Stay calm: "This matters to you. Help me understand what's driving the reaction."
options.c: Defend yourself firmly in the moment to establish that you won't be bullied in meetings.
options.d: Walk out of the meeting to avoid further escalation of the hostile dynamic with them.

question_text: You're aware of upcoming restructuring that will affect your team. Some team members are starting to ask if rumours are true. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Deny the rumours outright to maintain team stability through the uncertain period ahead.
options.b: Confirm what you know to maintain trust with the team during the uncertain period ahead.
options.c: Be honest about what you can and can't share now; commit to telling them as soon as possible.
options.d: Tell them to stop spreading rumours since they aren't helping the team's morale right now.

question_text: Christina Maslach's burnout research identifies three core dimensions of burnout:

options.a: Exhaustion, cynicism, and reduced professional efficacy.
options.b: Stress reaction, frustration, and chronic overwhelm signs.
options.c: Physical fatigue, emotional fatigue, and mental fatigue.
options.d: Anger, anxiety, and depressive symptoms taken together.

question_text: Research on recovery from work strain identifies which combination as MOST effective?

options.a: More sleep alone is sufficient to recover from any work strain.
options.b: Intense exercise to compensate for mental fatigue from work.
options.c: Disengagement, relaxation, mastery activities, and autonomy.
options.d: Complete inactivity to allow the body to fully restore itself.

question_text: Research on habit formation (Lally et al. 2009) found that on average, a new behaviour becomes habitual after approximately:

options.a: 66 days, with significant variation by behaviour type.
options.b: 21 days, regardless of the behaviour being established.
options.c: 30 days for almost any new behaviour pattern.
options.d: 6 months minimum to establish any new habit.

question_text: You feel you've stopped growing in your current role. The MOST constructive response is:

options.a: Wait for the next promotion cycle in the hope that a new role will create growth again.
options.b: Accept that mid-career plateaus are normal and just continue performing in the current role.
options.c: Diagnose what's stalled; design action: stretch, lateral move, mentor; talk to manager openly.
options.d: Start looking for external opportunities at other companies to find your next stretch role.

question_text: Your family is struggling with your work hours. The MOST sustainable response is:

options.a: Reduce work hours immediately, taking whatever career hit that decision creates for you.
options.b: Direct chat at home and work; redesign schedule rather than just adding more personal capacity.
options.c: Tell them this is the demand of mid-career life and they need to adjust accordingly.
options.d: Promise to do better at home and try to manage with more efficiency in your existing hours.

question_text: You realise you have been burned out for over a year — chronic exhaustion, cynicism, reduced effectiveness. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Look for a new job since the current one is probably the wrong fit for you right now.
options.b: Take a real break — vacation or sabbatical — to recover and identify structural changes.
options.c: Try meditation and exercise to rebuild your energy gradually over the coming months ahead.
options.d: Push through — this is what mid-career professional resilience really looks like in practice.

question_text: You realise the career you've built is no longer what you want. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Continue the current career and just tolerate the dissatisfaction since change is too hard.
options.b: Make a dramatic change immediately — quit your current role and pivot to something new now.
options.c: Explore: what's not working, what would, what's possible; small tests; 1-2 year transition.
options.d: Talk to a career coach to help you find a new direction that suits where you are now.

question_text: Your social media feed keeps showing peers' apparent successes — promotions, awards, expensive purchases. It is affecting your mood. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Quit all social media entirely to remove the source of the comparison from your daily life.
options.b: Recognise the comparison trap: curate feed, reduce passive scrolling, focus on your own goals.
options.c: Work harder to try to match the successes of your peers that you see in your feed.
options.d: Post more of your own successes to balance the comparison happening on the platform.

question_text: You've been feeling persistently low, with low energy and loss of interest, for 4+ weeks. The MOST appropriate action is:

options.a: Take a long vacation to reset and recover from the period of low mood you've been having.
options.b: Try more meditation and exercise to rebuild your wellbeing over the coming weeks ahead.
options.c: Push through and wait for the low mood to pass naturally over the coming weeks ahead.
options.d: Talk to a trusted person and engage a mental health professional; early help is better.

question_text: Your manager has been sending late-night requests for 3 months. You're exhausted. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Direct chat: "After-hours is affecting effectiveness. Can we manage urgency better?"
options.b: Stop responding after 8pm and let your manager notice the new pattern over the coming weeks.
options.c: Tolerate it — managers' demands often need to trump personal boundaries in mid-career roles.
options.d: Look for a new job at a different company since the pattern is unlikely to change here.

question_text: You realise you've been adding commitments faster than you've been removing them. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Work harder to keep up with everything that has been added to your plate over recent months.
options.b: Take a vacation to reset and recover from the buildup before continuing as before afterwards.
options.c: Hire help to handle some of the workload so you can keep all your existing commitments.
options.d: Audit commitments: cut, defer, renegotiate; honest conversations; add nothing new for 30 days.

question_text: Which is the MOST important practical limitation of current LLMs (like ChatGPT, Claude) for professional use?

options.a: They are too costly to use for routine professional purposes.
options.b: They work only in English and not in any other languages.
options.c: They can confidently produce false content requiring checks.
options.d: They are too slow to be useful in modern professional workflows.

question_text: Which is MOST effective when getting useful outputs from an AI assistant?

options.a: Send the same question multiple times to ensure accuracy.
options.b: Use the most formal language possible in your request.
options.c: Provide context, role, format, examples, and iterate.
options.d: Ask in a single short and direct question for clarity.

question_text: India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP), 2023, broadly:

options.a: Applies only to data held by Indian government departments.
options.b: Governs processing of personal data of individuals in India.
options.c: Affects only companies operating in the Indian IT services sector.
options.d: Has not yet been enacted into Indian law and is still pending.

question_text: The three major cloud platforms most commonly used by enterprises are:

options.a: Heroku, Vercel, Netlify Hosting.
options.b: AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud.
options.c: Dropbox, OneDrive, Google Drive Storage.
options.d: Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly Edge CDN.

question_text: Which of the following is the SINGLE most important cybersecurity practice for an individual professional?

options.a: Two-factor authentication plus a password manager.
options.b: Using up-to-date antivirus software on all devices.
options.c: Avoiding the use of public Wi-Fi networks entirely.
options.d: Regularly clearing browser history and cookies.

question_text: In data interpretation, the difference between correlation and causation is:

options.a: Correlation shows movement together; causation shows cause-and-effect.
options.b: Correlation is a statistical concept; causation is purely anecdotal.
options.c: Correlation and causation are essentially the same in business contexts.
options.d: Causation is simply a stronger form of correlation between variables.

question_text: A 'p-value' in statistical testing represents:

options.a: Size of the effect being measured by the test.
options.b: Probability that the research hypothesis is true.
options.c: Probability of the data given the null hypothesis.
options.d: Percentage of certainty in the resulting test.

question_text: In modern professional work, the MOST effective use of AI productivity tools is:

options.a: Augmenting human judgment on tasks with verifiable outputs.
options.b: Limiting use to creative tasks like writing and ideation.
options.c: Restricting use to low-stakes work where errors don't matter.
options.d: Replacing human work entirely wherever that is feasible.

question_text: 'Digital transformation' in business is best characterised as:

options.a: Hiring more software engineers across the entire organisation.
options.b: Rethinking business models through digital capabilities.
options.c: Adopting new software tools to do existing work faster.
options.d: Moving all of an organisation's data into the public cloud.

question_text: In Excel, INDEX/MATCH is often preferred over VLOOKUP because:

options.a: It is easier than VLOOKUP for beginners to learn and to use.
options.b: It is the only way to perform lookups in modern Excel today.
options.c: It can look up in any direction and resists column changes.
options.d: It is more accurate than VLOOKUP for numeric values overall.

question_text: In project management, 'scope creep' refers to:

options.a: Projects running over their originally scheduled completion time.
options.b: Gradual uncontrolled expansion of project scope beyond agreement.
options.c: Cost overruns that exceed the original budget allocated for work.
options.d: Team members taking on responsibilities outside their stated role.

question_text: A 'phishing' email typically attempts to:

options.a: Steal the physical hardware from the recipient.
options.b: Crash the recipient's computer with a malicious payload.
options.c: Send the recipient unsolicited targeted advertising.
options.d: Manipulate the recipient into revealing credentials.

question_text: In effective remote/hybrid work, the MOST important practice is:

options.a: Default to asynchronous, written communication for decisions.
options.b: Work the same hours as the headquarters location every day.
options.c: Use as many collaboration tools as possible across the team.
options.d: Hold more frequent meetings to maintain alignment across the team.

question_text: An API (Application Programming Interface) in business context allows:

options.a: Users to log in to multiple applications with one password.
options.b: One system to request data or functions from another programmatically.
options.c: Customers to access the company's database directly without permission.
options.d: Companies to share their source code with their partner organisations.

question_text: When evaluating a software vendor's security, 'encryption at rest' means:

options.a: Data is encrypted while stored in databases, files, and backups.
options.b: Data is encrypted only while being transmitted between systems.
options.c: Data is permanently encrypted and can never be accessed again.
options.d: Data is encrypted overnight and decrypted during work hours.

question_text: When evaluating a business analysis, the MOST important question to ask about the data is:

options.a: How was the data collected and is the sample representative?
options.b: Who collected the data and which department did they belong to?
options.c: How many data points were used in the underlying analysis?
options.d: What software was used to perform the analysis on the data?

question_text: Your team is debating whether to adopt AI tools widely. Some are enthusiastic, others resistant. The MOST effective leadership approach is:

options.a: Wait for industry standards to emerge before the team makes any adoption decisions on AI.
options.b: Pilot with willing subset, measure impact, share openly, then data-informed wider decisions.
options.c: Mandate adoption — the efficiency gains demand it across the team as soon as possible.
options.d: Allow voluntary adoption — let each person choose whether to use AI in their workflows.

question_text: You realise a colleague has been emailing customer data to their personal email to work on it at home. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Ignore the issue — they probably have a legitimate reason for working with the data at home.
options.b: Confront the colleague publicly to make sure others on the team learn from the situation.
options.c: Immediately raise with manager and security/compliance — this is a serious privacy violation.
options.d: Private chat with them and report through proper channels if the practice isn't resolved.

question_text: You've been using ChatGPT to help draft work products. Your company doesn't have a clear policy yet. The MOST appropriate approach is:

options.a: Stop using AI completely until the company has issued clear policies on what is allowed.
options.b: Continue using ChatGPT without disclosure since the company's policies aren't yet defined.
options.c: Be transparent: "I've been using AI for X. I verify outputs. Should I be more or less open?"
options.d: Use AI heavily without disclosure to maximise the productivity advantage while you can.

question_text: You receive an email that appears to be from your CEO asking you to urgently process a wire transfer to a new vendor. The MOST appropriate action is:

options.a: Process the transfer right away — the email is from the CEO and the request is urgent.
options.b: Verify through a separate channel: call the CEO on a known number, not one from the email.
options.c: Forward the email to the finance team for them to process the transfer on your behalf.
options.d: Reply to the email asking the CEO for confirmation before processing the wire transfer.

question_text: You're presenting analytics findings to a non-technical executive who likes to drill into details. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Send the raw data file in advance and discuss the findings in person with the executive.
options.b: Keep the presentation high-level and dismiss requests for the granular detail they like.
options.c: Show all charts in detail upfront to satisfy their stated preference for the granular data.
options.d: Lead with insight and recommendation, simple visuals, drill-down ready when they ask for it.

question_text: Your team uses a legacy system that frustrates everyone but switching would require significant investment. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Wait for the system to fail before pushing for change — at that point everyone will agree.
options.b: Quantify: cost of frustration, cost of switching, risk; business case in business language.
options.c: Accept the situation since you don't own the decision about which systems get replaced.
options.d: Push for replacement immediately — the frustration is a real cost for the whole team daily.

question_text: You used an AI tool to research a market data point and it gave you a confident-sounding statistic. The MOST appropriate use of that statistic is:

options.a: Use the statistic with a footnote citing the AI tool that you used as the original source.
options.b: Use it without citation since the AI tool is not really a primary research source itself.
options.c: Use it directly in your presentation — the AI tool is reliable for typical market statistics.
options.d: Use as a starting point; verify from authoritative sources before any consequential use.

question_text: A new productivity tool is being adopted across your industry. The MOST effective way to develop competence is:

options.a: Wait until you're forced to use it before investing time in learning the new tool well.
options.b: Watch demonstration videos online to get a feel for what the tool does before using it.
options.c: Identify a real problem; apply the tool to it; deepen through repeated application.
options.d: Take a structured course or read the full documentation first, then experiment with it.

question_text: I use AI tools sophisticatedly — providing structured prompts, verifying outputs, and integrating them into my work judiciously.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am comfortable interpreting data and making decisions informed by it.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can translate technical concepts into business language and vice versa.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am confident in my ability to learn new digital tools as my role demands.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I follow strong digital security practices — 2FA, password manager, careful with links and attachments.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am effective in async/written work environments, not just synchronous meetings.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am genuinely curious about how technology is changing my industry, even when it's not directly required for my work.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: When I see a data-driven claim, I instinctively ask how the data was collected and what its limitations are.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I tend to avoid learning new digital tools unless I'm forced to.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: How often do you read about technology developments affecting your industry?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you invest time to deepen your skills with tools you already use, rather than just learning new ones?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)
